1.title: Reflection- Jonathan Beauzile
paragraphs: On Monday, March 9, 2026, we arrived in Warsaw and began our trip by visiting the POLIN Museum of the History of Polish Jews. The focus of the day was learning about Jewish life in Poland before the Holocaust, which gave important context before studying the destruction that happened during World War II.

What surprised me most was how vibrant, established, and normal Jewish life in Poland was before the war. Before this trip, I think I subconsciously connected Jewish history in Poland mostly with suffering, loss, and the Holocaust. However, the POLIN Museum showed me that Jewish life in Poland was much more than tragedy. Jewish communities had existed there for centuries, building schools, businesses, religious traditions, cultural spaces, and everyday lives. They were not just victims in history. They were students, parents, workers, artists, leaders, and neighbors.

This changed the way I understood the Holocaust because it made me realize that the Nazis did not only take lives. They destroyed entire communities and worlds filled with identity, memory, culture, and meaning. Learning about Jewish life before the Holocaust is important because it humanizes the people who were lost. Instead of only thinking about statistics or historical facts, I was able to see Jewish people as individuals with families, dreams, routines, and futures that should have continued.

The museum helped me understand that Jewish life before the war was vibrant and diverse, not one-dimensional. Seeing this made the history feel more personal and more tragic because I understood more deeply what was actually taken away.

2.title: Day 2 Reflection
paragraphs: On the second day of our trip, March 10th was a time to remember and reflect on how many lives were changed. From meeting with members of the Polish Jewish community, hearing about the courageous acts of the Righteous Among the Nations, from touring the Jewish Historical Institute and walking along parts of the former Warsaw Ghetto, it really hit that the Holocaust wasn't just a tragedy that is counted in numbers but rather in lives lost, communities destroyed and stories lost. As we stood by all of the memorial plaques and preserved remains of what once was the Ghetto, we realized that there is such great value in keeping memories alive and making sure the voices of those who suffered and those who resisted do not die.

3.title: Day 3 Reflection
paragraphs: On March 10th our tour of the former Majdanek Concentration Camp and current Museum/Memorial Site, was an emotionally draining and eye opening experience. We were able to walk through the barracks, watch towers, gas chambers and memorial areas such as “The Prisoners at Majdanek”, the column of the three eagles and we ended in front of the crematorium and Mausoleum which contained the ashes of the victim’s. The final image left a profound sense of sorrow and contemplation. As the first concentration camp we toured, this visit had an incredible long term effect on all of us in terms of how much we understood what the true cost of lives were during the Holocaust.

6.title: Day 6 Reflection
paragraphs: On day 6 of our trip we learned about the Jewish Community Center of Kraków and the Lancut Synagogue. The Jewish Community Center of Kraków and the endurance of the Lancut synagogue both communicate the resilience of the Jewish people and are a testament to how they always get back up no matter what stands in their way. The Jewish Community Center of Kraków has helped rebuild the Jewish community in Poland, bringing people back to their Jewish roots. Additionally, the Lancut synagogue survived the invasion of the Nazis, who ended up destroying most other places of Jewish relation within Poland, truly showing its endurance.
